title: Alsine douglasii
intro: Alsine douglasii, also known as Douglas' pearlwort or Douglas' crowfoot, is a species of flowering plant in the family Caryophyllaceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including the treatment of gastrointestinal issues, such as diarrhea and dyspepsia, and its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Alsine douglasii include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. Traditionally, the plant is made into teas, infusions, and tinctures, with the most common herbal preparations being a decoction or a combination with other herbs, such as peppermint or chamomile, to enhance its therapeutic properties.
taxonomy: Alsine douglasii is classified within the family Caryophyllaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the pink or carnation family. This classification places Alsine douglasii in the order Caryophyllales, which is a large and diverse order of flowering plants. Alsine douglasii is further classified as a member of the genus Alsine, which is a group of flowering plants that are primarily found in the Northern Hemisphere. The species Alsine douglasii is native to North America, where it can be found growing in a variety of habitats. Alsine douglasii is a dicotyledonous plant, meaning that it has two cotyledons or seed leaves when it germinates.
